name: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 09.03.2026

origin.filename: Minutes of the meeting of CWWG as on 09.03.2026.pdf

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: Key Insights
texts.text: Key Insights

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Rabi Season harvest and Summer Season Sowing Status: Summer sowing data collection has been started from 9 th  February, 2026 and the sowing is in preliminary stage. As on March 06, 2026, the area sown under summer crops is 31.69 lakh ha as compared to 33.38 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.  Of this, area of 25.30 lakh ha has been sown under Rice this year.
texts.text: Rabi Season harvest and Summer Season Sowing Status: Summer sowing data collection has been started from 9 th  February, 2026 and the sowing is in preliminary stage. As on March 06, 2026, the area sown under summer crops is 31.69 lakh ha as compared to 33.38 lakh ha during the corresponding period of last year.  Of this, area of 25.30 lakh ha has been sown under Rice this year.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  As  on  06.03.2026, 8.64%  of area has been harvested  for Rabi crops all over India. Approximately 20% area of Pulses mainly in Gram (21%) & Urdbean (46%); 15.51% area of Oilseeds  majorly  in  Rapeseed  &  Mustard  (16.57%);  12.04%  area  of  Shri  Anna  cum  Coarse Cereals majorly in Jowar (15.94%) and 16.74% area of Rice has been harvested for rabi crops 2025-26.
texts.text:  As  on  06.03.2026, 8.64%  of area has been harvested  for Rabi crops all over India. Approximately 20% area of Pulses mainly in Gram (21%) & Urdbean (46%); 15.51% area of Oilseeds  majorly  in  Rapeseed  &  Mustard  (16.57%);  12.04%  area  of  Shri  Anna  cum  Coarse Cereals majorly in Jowar (15.94%) and 16.74% area of Rice has been harvested for rabi crops 2025-26.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2. Weather Update :
texts.text: 2. Weather Update :

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Temperature: Maximum  Temperature  is  likely  to  be  above  normal  in  most  parts  of  India; except Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, North Interior Karnataka and Kerala.
texts.text: Temperature: Maximum  Temperature  is  likely  to  be  above  normal  in  most  parts  of  India; except Madhya Pradesh, Maharashtra, North Interior Karnataka and Kerala.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a) Rainfall Distribution: From March 01- March 09 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is 95%  lower  than  its  Long  Period  Average  (LPA),  with  regional  variations:  (i)  East  & Northeast India: (-)  85%; (ii)  Northwest India:  (-) 99%;  (iii)  Central India:  (-)  100%;  and South Peninsula: (-) 89%.
texts.text: Rainfall Distribution: From March 01- March 09 2026, the All-India cumulative rainfall is 95%  lower  than  its  Long  Period  Average  (LPA),  with  regional  variations:  (i)  East  & Northeast India: (-)  85%; (ii)  Northwest India:  (-) 99%;  (iii)  Central India:  (-)  100%;  and South Peninsula: (-) 89%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Under the influence of fresh Western Disturbances, scattered light rainfall/snowfall likely over Western Himalayan Region during 09th-11th March.
texts.text:  Under the influence of fresh Western Disturbances, scattered light rainfall/snowfall likely over Western Himalayan Region during 09th-11th March.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Isolated  light  rainfall/snowfall  likely  over  Jammu-Kashmir,  with  the  possibility  of increase  to  scattered  to  fairly  widespread,  light  to  moderate  rainfall/snowfall  during 10th-12th  and  decrease  to  Isolated  light  rainfall/snowfall  on  13th  and  14th  March. Isolated  to  scattered  light  rainfall/snowfall  also  likely  over  Himachal  Pradesh  and Uttarakhand during 10th -14th March.
texts.text:  Isolated  light  rainfall/snowfall  likely  over  Jammu-Kashmir,  with  the  possibility  of increase  to  scattered  to  fairly  widespread,  light  to  moderate  rainfall/snowfall  during 10th-12th  and  decrease  to  Isolated  light  rainfall/snowfall  on  13th  and  14th  March. Isolated  to  scattered  light  rainfall/snowfall  also  likely  over  Himachal  Pradesh  and Uttarakhand during 10th -14th March.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Isolated thunderstorm, lightning & gusty winds likely over Jammu-Kashmir on 09th & 10th and over Himachal Pradesh on 11th & 12th March. Isolated to scattered light/moderate  rainfall  with  thunderstorm,  lightning  &  gusty  winds  likely  over  East India during 09th-11th March and over Northeast India during 10th-14th March.
texts.text:  Isolated thunderstorm, lightning & gusty winds likely over Jammu-Kashmir on 09th & 10th and over Himachal Pradesh on 11th & 12th March. Isolated to scattered light/moderate  rainfall  with  thunderstorm,  lightning  &  gusty  winds  likely  over  East India during 09th-11th March and over Northeast India during 10th-14th March.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Heat wave to severe heat wave conditions very likely to continue in isolated pockets over Himachal Pradesh on 09th March.
texts.text:  Heat wave to severe heat wave conditions very likely to continue in isolated pockets over Himachal Pradesh on 09th March.

texts.orig: Agricultural Statistics Division| Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare |Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare
texts.text: Agricultural Statistics Division| Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare |Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Day  temperatures  likely  to  continue  to  remain  above  normal  by  5-8°C  over  JammuKashmir  &  Himachal  Pradesh;  by  3-5°C  over  remaining  parts  of  plains  of  Northwest India during next 3 days and decrease thereafter by 2-3°C during subsequent 3 days.
texts.text:  Day  temperatures  likely  to  continue  to  remain  above  normal  by  5-8°C  over  JammuKashmir  &  Himachal  Pradesh;  by  3-5°C  over  remaining  parts  of  plains  of  Northwest India during next 3 days and decrease thereafter by 2-3°C during subsequent 3 days.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3. Inputs Situation
texts.text: 3. Inputs Situation

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: a) Water Reservoir Status : As on 05 March 2026, the total live water storage in 166 important reservoirs across the country (monitored by CWC) is 104.133 billion cubic meter, which is 57%  of  total  live  storage  capacity  (183.565  BCM).  The  current  year's  storage  is  nearly 112.86 percent of last year's storage and 126.84 percent of the average storage of the last ten years during the corresponding period. The overall storage position is better than the corresponding period of last year in the country as a whole and is also better than the Normal storage during the corresponding period.
texts.text: Water Reservoir Status : As on 05 March 2026, the total live water storage in 166 important reservoirs across the country (monitored by CWC) is 104.133 billion cubic meter, which is 57%  of  total  live  storage  capacity  (183.565  BCM).  The  current  year's  storage  is  nearly 112.86 percent of last year's storage and 126.84 percent of the average storage of the last ten years during the corresponding period. The overall storage position is better than the corresponding period of last year in the country as a whole and is also better than the Normal storage during the corresponding period.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: b) Groundwater  Levels :  In  Post-monsoon  2025,  around  90%  of  the  country's  monitoring stations  exhibited  a  depth  to  water  level  within  10  meters  below  ground  level.  Deeper water  levels  of  more  than  20  m  are  observed  in  around  5%  of  stations  of  the  country covering  mainly  the western  states,  especially  Western  Rajasthan,  Punjab,  Haryana  and Chandigarh.
texts.text: Groundwater  Levels :  In  Post-monsoon  2025,  around  90%  of  the  country's  monitoring stations  exhibited  a  depth  to  water  level  within  10  meters  below  ground  level.  Deeper water  levels  of  more  than  20  m  are  observed  in  around  5%  of  stations  of  the  country covering  mainly  the western  states,  especially  Western  Rajasthan,  Punjab,  Haryana  and Chandigarh.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh  during  post-monsoon  2025  has  been  significantly  influenced  by  the  over extraction of groundwater compared to the recharge during the monsoon period.
texts.text:  The deeper groundwater level in the states like Western Rajasthan, Punjab, Haryana and Chandigarh  during  post-monsoon  2025  has  been  significantly  influenced  by  the  over extraction of groundwater compared to the recharge during the monsoon period.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  Annual  water  level  comparison  with  the  previous  year  Post-monsoon  2024  to  Postmonsoon  2025  has  shown  that  about  63%  of  total  analyzed  stations  of  the  country experienced rise in  annual  water level fluctuation may  be  attributed to the less groundwater extraction compared to 2024.
texts.text:  Annual  water  level  comparison  with  the  previous  year  Post-monsoon  2024  to  Postmonsoon  2025  has  shown  that  about  63%  of  total  analyzed  stations  of  the  country experienced rise in  annual  water level fluctuation may  be  attributed to the less groundwater extraction compared to 2024.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  About 73% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level  in  the  decadal  mean  water  level  fluctuation  of  2015-2024  with  respect  to  Postmonsoon 2025.
texts.text:  About 73% of the analyzed stations of the unconfined aquifer experienced a rise of water level  in  the  decadal  mean  water  level  fluctuation  of  2015-2024  with  respect  to  Postmonsoon 2025.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig:  The  Groundwater  Drought  Indices  indicate  that  in Post-monsoon  2025 ,  groundwater conditions  across  India  remained  largely  stable,  with 91%  of  monitoring  stations showing no  deficit .  However,  localized  deficits  were  observed  in  several  regions  notably in Meghalaya, Nagaland, and the Dadar Nagar Havili & Daman & Diu.
texts.text:  The  Groundwater  Drought  Indices  indicate  that  in Post-monsoon  2025 ,  groundwater conditions  across  India  remained  largely  stable,  with 91%  of  monitoring  stations showing no  deficit .  However,  localized  deficits  were  observed  in  several  regions  notably in Meghalaya, Nagaland, and the Dadar Nagar Havili & Daman & Diu.

texts.text: Agricultural Statistics Division| Department of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare |Ministry of Agriculture & Farmers Welfare

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: c) Fertilizers : The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products.
texts.text: Fertilizers : The fertilizer supply remains stable across all products.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. Pests & Diseases : Intensity of pests and diseases at some fields has been reported to be above the Economic Threshold Level for Citrus (Gummosis) and Chilli (Leaf curl) in Maharashtra. The  Advisory  has  been  issued  by  the  survey  team  to  the  farms  and  regular  monitoring  is being carried out. The overall intensity of pests and diseases remained below the Economic Threshold Level.
texts.text: Pests & Diseases : Intensity of pests and diseases at some fields has been reported to be above the Economic Threshold Level for Citrus (Gummosis) and Chilli (Leaf curl) in Maharashtra. The  Advisory  has  been  issued  by  the  survey  team  to  the  farms  and  regular  monitoring  is being carried out. The overall intensity of pests and diseases remained below the Economic Threshold Level.
